name: Strawberries
description: Sweet, juicy berries at their best from late spring into early summer.
storage_tips: Store unwashed in a breathable container. Keep chilled and use within 2-4 days.

name: Blueberries
description: Bright, aromatic berries that peak through midsummer.
storage_tips: Keep refrigerated and dry. Use within 3-7 days for best flavor.

name: Peaches
description: Stone fruit with fragrant flavor, best from summer's warm stretch.
storage_tips: Ripen at room temperature, then refrigerate. Use within 3-5 days after ripening.

name: Apples
description: Crisp, storage-friendly fruit that shines in early fall.
storage_tips: Store cool and dry. Many varieties keep 4-8 weeks in the fridge.

name: Pears
description: Sweet-tart fruit with a gentle texture, commonly best in early to mid fall.
storage_tips: Ripen at room temperature, then refrigerate. Eat when slightly soft to the touch.

name: Cherries
description: Rich, tangy fruit that arrives in summer and fades quickly.
storage_tips: Refrigerate in a single layer when possible. Use within 2-5 days.

name: Asparagus
description: Tender spring spears; best when they're young and crisp.
storage_tips: Store upright with a little water in the fridge. Use within 3-5 days.

name: Spinach
description: Cool-weather leafy green with a mild, sweet taste early in the season.
storage_tips: Store wrapped (or in a container) with a paper towel to absorb moisture. Use within 5-7 days.

name: Lettuce
description: Crunchy salad greens that tend to be most flavorful in spring.
storage_tips: Keep cold and slightly dry. Store in a breathable container and use within a week.

name: Tomatoes
description: Juicy summer fruit (botanically) at peak flavor in midsummer through early fall.
storage_tips: Ripen at room temperature, then refrigerate if needed. Avoid chilling unripe tomatoes.

name: Sweet Corn
description: Fresh, sweet kernels best right after harvest.
storage_tips: Refrigerate promptly and use within 1-3 days. Keep the husks on for freshness.

name: Peppers
description: From mild to spicy—best when warm-season crops are thriving.
storage_tips: Store in the crisper drawer. Use within 5-10 days for best texture.

name: Cucumbers
description: Refreshing crunch most commonly available in peak summer months.
storage_tips: Refrigerate in a breathable bag. Use within 3-7 days.

name: Broccoli
description: Tender florets that often peak from late summer into fall.
storage_tips: Store unwashed wrapped in a bag. Use within 4-7 days.

name: Basil
description: Aromatic herb that thrives in warm weather.
storage_tips: Treat like fresh flowers (trim ends, add a little water) or store loosely wrapped in the fridge. Use within 3-5 days.

name: Cilantro
description: Bright, citrusy herb best fresh during the growing season.
storage_tips: Store stems down in a jar with a bit of water, loosely covered. Use within 3-7 days.

name: Parsley
description: Versatile herb that stays useful from spring through early fall.
storage_tips: Store like cilantro (jar + water) or wrap lightly and refrigerate. Use within 5-10 days.

name: Morel Mushrooms
description: Prized spring wild mushrooms—high season is short and temperature-driven.
storage_tips: Clean gently (don't soak). Cook soon after purchase; refrigerate briefly if needed.

name: Chanterelles
description: Golden forest mushrooms with a savory, fruity aroma, common in midsummer.
storage_tips: Brush clean and refrigerate in a breathable container. Use within 2-4 days.

name: Oyster Mushrooms
description: Tender mushrooms with a mild flavor that can appear in multiple warm periods.
storage_tips: Keep dry in the fridge and cook within a few days for best texture.

name: Oysters
description: Cold-water bivalves; availability is seasonal in many coastal regions.
storage_tips: Keep refrigerated and on ice if possible. Consume quickly after purchase.

name: Bluefish
description: Bold-flavored fish that often shows up in warmer-month landings.
storage_tips: Keep cold immediately. Cook soon after purchase for best flavor.

name: Fluke
description: A flatfish that's commonly available across late winter into spring.
storage_tips: Store cold and cook within 1-2 days. Check for clean, mild ocean smell.

name: Venison (Deer)
description: Lean, flavorful game meat that's commonly available in fall and winter seasons.
storage_tips: Keep frozen or refrigerated short-term. Cook to a safe internal temperature; rest briefly before slicing.

name: Wild Turkey
description: Game bird availability often varies by spring and fall seasons.
storage_tips: Store cold promptly. Roast or braise for tenderness; avoid overcooking.

name: Rabbit
description: A tender game meat that tends to show up most in cool seasons.
storage_tips: Keep refrigerated and cook soon (or freeze). Braise can help keep it moist.

name: Watermelon
description: Large, refreshing summer melon at its sweetest in July and August.
storage_tips: Store whole at room temperature. Once cut, refrigerate and use within 3-5 days.

name: Cantaloupe
description: Fragrant melon with soft orange flesh, best in mid-to-late summer.
storage_tips: Ripen at room temperature until fragrant. Refrigerate once cut and use within 3 days.

name: Raspberries
description: Delicate berries with intense flavor, peaking in early to midsummer.
storage_tips: Handle gently. Refrigerate and eat within 1-3 days. Do not wash until ready to eat.

name: Blackberries
description: Dark, bold-flavored berries that thrive in summer heat.
storage_tips: Refrigerate in a single layer. Use within 2-3 days for best quality.

name: Plums
description: Sweet-tart stone fruit available through midsummer into early fall.
storage_tips: Ripen at room temperature, then refrigerate. Use within 3-5 days once ripe.

name: Nectarines
description: Smooth-skinned stone fruit with juicy flavor similar to peaches.
storage_tips: Ripen at room temperature, then refrigerate. Use within 3-5 days after ripening.

name: Cranberries
description: Tart, ruby-red berries harvested in fall bogs.
storage_tips: Refrigerate in their original bag. Fresh cranberries keep 3-4 weeks; freeze for longer storage.

name: Grapes
description: Table and wine grapes at their best from late summer through early fall.
storage_tips: Refrigerate unwashed in a ventilated bag. Use within 1-2 weeks.

name: Rhubarb
description: Tart stalks used in pies and preserves, harvested in spring.
storage_tips: Wrap stalks in foil or plastic and refrigerate. Use within 1-2 weeks.

name: Figs
description: Soft, honey-sweet fruit with a short late-summer harvest window.
storage_tips: Extremely perishable. Refrigerate and eat within 1-2 days of purchase.

name: Zucchini
description: Prolific summer squash that's best when small and tender.
storage_tips: Refrigerate in a plastic bag. Use within 4-5 days.

name: Summer Squash
description: Tender-skinned squashes abundant from midsummer through early fall.
storage_tips: Store in the crisper drawer. Use within 4-5 days while still firm.

name: Winter Squash
description: Hard-skinned squash varieties (butternut, acorn, etc.) harvested in fall.
storage_tips: Store in a cool, dry place. Keeps 1-3 months depending on variety.

name: Pumpkins
description: Iconic fall harvest, used for both cooking and decoration.
storage_tips: Store in a cool, dry place away from direct sunlight. Keeps 2-3 months.

name: Eggplant
description: Warm-season nightshade with rich, meaty flesh when grilled or roasted.
storage_tips: Store at room temperature if using soon, or refrigerate for up to a week.

name: Green Beans
description: Crisp snap beans abundant from midsummer through early fall.
storage_tips: Refrigerate unwashed in a bag. Use within 5-7 days.

name: Peas
description: Sweet spring legume best eaten soon after picking.
storage_tips: Refrigerate in their pods and use within 2-3 days. Sugar converts to starch quickly.

name: Beets
description: Earthy root vegetable with two harvest windows: spring and fall.
storage_tips: Remove greens, store roots in a bag in the fridge. Keeps 2-4 weeks.

name: Carrots
description: Sweet, crunchy root vegetable best from late summer through fall frost.
storage_tips: Remove greens, store in a bag in the fridge. Keeps 3-4 weeks.

name: Radishes
description: Peppery spring root ready in just a few weeks from planting.
storage_tips: Remove greens, store in a bag in the fridge. Use within 1-2 weeks.

name: Cabbage
description: Hardy brassica with spring and fall harvest windows.
storage_tips: Refrigerate in a plastic bag. Keeps 1-2 months.

name: Kale
description: Hardy green that thrives in cool weather and sweetens after frost.
storage_tips: Wrap in a damp towel, refrigerate. Use within 5-7 days.

name: Swiss Chard
description: Colorful leafy green available from late spring through fall.
storage_tips: Store in a bag with a damp paper towel. Use within 3-5 days.

name: Cauliflower
description: Mild brassica best in cool weather—spring and fall harvests.
storage_tips: Refrigerate in a loosely sealed bag. Use within 4-7 days.

name: Potatoes
description: Versatile tuber harvested from midsummer through fall; stores well.
storage_tips: Store in a cool, dark, dry place. Keep away from onions. Lasts several weeks to months.

name: Sweet Potatoes
description: Warm-weather tuber harvested in fall before first frost.
storage_tips: Store in a cool, dark place (not the fridge). Keeps 1-2 months after curing.

name: Onions
description: Foundation vegetable harvested in summer; cured bulbs store through winter.
storage_tips: Store in a cool, dry, well-ventilated spot. Keeps 1-3 months depending on variety.

name: Garlic
description: Planted in fall, harvested midsummer. Fresh garlic has a milder bite.
storage_tips: Store in a cool, dry place with good airflow. Cured garlic keeps 3-6 months.

name: Leeks
description: Mild allium that thrives in cool weather with a long harvest window.
storage_tips: Wrap in a damp paper towel and refrigerate. Use within 1-2 weeks.

name: Turnips
description: Mild root vegetable at its best in spring and fall.
storage_tips: Remove greens, refrigerate roots in a bag. Keeps 2-3 weeks.

name: Parsnips
description: Sweet, starchy root that improves after frost.
storage_tips: Refrigerate in a bag. Keeps 2-4 weeks. Sweetens with cold storage.

name: Brussels Sprouts
description: Nutty mini-cabbages that sweeten dramatically after fall frost.
storage_tips: Refrigerate unwashed in a bag. Use within 3-5 days for best flavor.

name: Celery
description: Crisp stalks harvested from midsummer through fall.
storage_tips: Wrap in foil and refrigerate. Keeps 1-2 weeks.

name: Dill
description: Feathery herb with a fresh, anise-like flavor popular with fish and pickles.
storage_tips: Wrap in a damp paper towel and refrigerate. Use within 3-5 days.

name: Mint
description: Vigorous herb available fresh throughout the growing season.
storage_tips: Store stems in a glass of water at room temperature or loosely wrap and refrigerate. Use within a week.

name: Rosemary
description: Woody, aromatic evergreen herb that can be harvested year-round in milder areas.
storage_tips: Wrap in a damp paper towel and refrigerate. Keeps 1-2 weeks.

name: Thyme
description: Earthy, savory herb available fresh from late spring through fall.
storage_tips: Refrigerate loosely wrapped. Use within 1-2 weeks.

name: Sage
description: Earthy, savory herb with velvety leaves, harvest peaks in summer.
storage_tips: Wrap in a damp paper towel and refrigerate. Use within a week.

name: Chives
description: Mild onion-flavored herb, one of the first to appear in spring.
storage_tips: Wrap in a damp paper towel and refrigerate. Use within a week.

name: Oregano
description: Mediterranean herb that intensifies in flavor during warm months.
storage_tips: Refrigerate fresh sprigs wrapped loosely. Use within 4-7 days.

name: Maitake (Hen of the Woods)
description: Large, clustered fall mushroom found at the base of oak trees.
storage_tips: Refrigerate in a paper bag. Use within 3-5 days. Tear into pieces rather than cutting.

name: Chicken of the Woods
description: Bright orange shelf fungus with a meaty texture, found in summer and fall.
storage_tips: Refrigerate and use within 2-3 days. Best when young and tender at the edges.

name: Lion's Mane
description: Shaggy white mushroom with a crabmeat-like flavor, found in late summer and fall.
storage_tips: Refrigerate in a paper bag. Use within 3-5 days. Sauté until golden.

name: Black Trumpet
description: Dark, thin-fleshed forest mushroom with an intensely earthy, smoky flavor.
storage_tips: Brush clean gently. Refrigerate and use within 3-4 days. Dries exceptionally well.

name: Porcini
description: Prized bolete with rich, nutty flavor found in late summer and fall forests.
storage_tips: Use quickly (within 1-2 days). Brush clean, don't soak. Slice thin to check for worms.

name: Giant Puffball
description: Massive round mushroom found in meadows and forest edges in late summer.
storage_tips: Use within 1-2 days. Slice and cook; interior should be pure white when fresh.

name: Blue Crab
description: Sweet, succulent crab abundant along the mid-Atlantic coast in summer.
storage_tips: Keep alive and cold until cooking. Cook same day as purchase if possible.

name: Lobster
description: Premium crustacean with peak catches in summer along New England coasts.
storage_tips: Keep alive in the fridge covered with a damp towel. Cook the same day.

name: Clams
description: Hard-shell and soft-shell varieties available in cooler months along the coast.
storage_tips: Store in the fridge in a bowl covered with a damp cloth. Use within 1-2 days.

name: Scallops (Sea)
description: Sweet, tender bivalve harvested in cooler months from Atlantic waters.
storage_tips: Refrigerate on ice and use within 1-2 days. Pat dry before searing.

name: Mussels
description: Affordable bivalve available year-round but best in colder months.
storage_tips: Store in the fridge in an open container with a damp cloth. Use within 1-2 days.

name: Striped Bass
description: Prized game fish with flaky white flesh, migrating along the coast spring through fall.
storage_tips: Keep cold on ice. Cook within 1-2 days of purchase.

name: Flounder
description: Mild, delicate flatfish available through much of the year in the Northeast.
storage_tips: Refrigerate and cook within 1-2 days. Very delicate; handle gently.

name: Squid
description: Versatile cephalopod caught in warmer months along the Atlantic.
storage_tips: Clean and cook the same day, or freeze immediately. Cook quickly (under 2 min) or braise long.

name: Black Sea Bass
description: Firm, white-fleshed fish found along the mid-Atlantic coast in warmer months.
storage_tips: Refrigerate on ice and cook within 1-2 days.

name: Cod
description: Cold-water whitefish traditionally important in New England fisheries.
storage_tips: Keep very cold. Cook within 1-2 days. Firm flesh holds up well to baking and frying.

name: Tautog (Blackfish)
description: Reef-dwelling fish with firm, white flesh, caught in spring and fall.
storage_tips: Refrigerate and cook within 1-2 days.

name: Scallops (Bay)
description: Small, sweet scallops harvested in fall and winter from bays and estuaries.
storage_tips: Refrigerate on ice. Use within 1-2 days. Best pan-seared or eaten raw.

name: Tuna
description: Large pelagic fish caught offshore in warmer months.
storage_tips: Keep very cold on ice. Sushi-grade should be consumed same day; cook-grade within 1-2 days.

name: Duck (Wild)
description: Waterfowl available during fall and winter hunting seasons.
storage_tips: Refrigerate or freeze promptly. Rich flavor pairs well with fruit-based sauces.

name: Goose (Wild)
description: Waterfowl hunted primarily in fall and winter migratory seasons.
storage_tips: Freeze if not cooking within a day. Roast low and slow to render fat.

name: Squirrel
description: Small game animal hunted in fall with mild, lean meat.
storage_tips: Refrigerate and cook promptly. Braise or stew for tenderness.

name: Ruffed Grouse
description: Woodland game bird with delicate white meat, hunted in fall.
storage_tips: Refrigerate and cook within 1-2 days. Gentle cooking preserves moisture.

name: Black Bear
description: Large game animal with rich, dark meat; carefully regulated hunting season.
storage_tips: Must be cooked to 160°F internal temperature. Freeze portions not used immediately.
